content:
{
  "title": "Adversarially Conditioned Prompt Rewriting for Robust Language Model Defense",
  "problem": "Current large language models are vulnerable to adversarial jailbreak prompts that bypass safety filters and elicit harmful or restricted outputs. There is a need for prompting strategies that enhance model robustness against such attacks.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes and open-domain settings, adversarial prompt attacks threaten user safety and trust. Existing filtering and fine-tuning approaches are limited by their static or model-specific nature. Prompt-level interventions offer a model-agnostic and deployable defense layer, but current methods lack systematic adversarial conditioning.",
  "method": "We propose Adversarially Conditioned Prompt Rewriting (ACPR), a method that dynamically augments user prompts with learned adversarial-aware context. The rewriting module is trained via reinforcement learning using a reward signal that penalizes successful jailbreaks and rewards safe completions. At inference, input prompts are automatically rewritten with context that both preserves intent and steers the LLM away from adversarial interpretations, acting as a preemptive defense regardless of attack strategy.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate ACPR on the AdvBench and JailbreakBench datasets, comparing against strong baselines: vanilla prompting, OpenAI Moderation, and defense-tuned models (e.g., Constitutional AI prompts). Metrics will include jailbreak success rate, output harmlessness (via human and automated classifiers), and prompt fidelity. A conclusive experiment is a head-to-head A/B test on novel, unseen jailbreak attacks, demonstrating ACPR reduces attack success without degrading benign prompt utility."
}
